followup_notes.changes_from_prior_cycle: Revised novelty score upward from 6 to 7, crediting the assembled full-pipeline package, Bayesian application, and GW250114 demonstration as more than just the phase correction engineering. Codex's clearer articulation of the frequency-cutoff/start-time analogy prompted me to strengthen that objection in my review. The fixed-extrinsic-parameter issue is maintained at moderate severity (not elevated to major as Codex suggests) because the partial sensitivity check provides some mitigation. Overall score and verdict unchanged at 7/10 and 'promising'. Added a new minor objection about the bibliographic error in Ref. [107] (YASTN cited instead of numpy) that all reviewers missed.
invalid_points_from_other_reviewers: Gemini's novelty score of 9 and description as 'highly novel' overstates the advance. The paper builds directly on Ref. [113] (Dec 2025, same group) for the amplitude model and Ref. [111] (March 2026, same group) for the theoretical grounding. The phase model is empirical. A score of 7 is more appropriate.

Codex's elevation of the fixed-extrinsic-parameter issue to 'major' with would_change_verdict_if_true=true is too severe. The paper explicitly checks inclination and polarization sampling with negligible impact, and for GW250114 the sky localization is tightly constrained from the full event. This is moderate, not major.

Codex's 'major' rating for nuisance parameter degeneracies (would_change_verdict_if_true=true) overstates the severity. The paper explicitly frames this as a consistency test, not a definitive beyond-GR probe. The degeneracy concern is legitimate but does not rise to verdict-changing given the stated scope of the test.

Gemini's technical soundness score of 8 is too generous given: (1) the phase model has no first-principles derivation, (2) the key theoretical reference [111] is an unreviewed companion preprint by the same group, and (3) the real-data comparison has the fixed-extrinsic-parameter limitation. A score of 7 is more calibrated.
valid_points_from_other_reviewers: Codex correctly identifies that the frequency lower cutoff plays an analogous role to the ringdown start time, making the 'avoids start-time systematic' claim overstated. This is more crisply stated in Codex's review than in my cycle 1 version.

Codex's objection that the unweighted mismatch metric (Eq. 6) is inconsistent with the noise-weighted indistinguishability criterion invoked in the Discussion is a legitimate technical inconsistency I had flagged only as a spot-check item.

Both Codex and Gemini correctly note that the phenomenological nuisance parameters (A_ext, γ, φ₁) could absorb degeneracies or beyond-GR features, making the GR test less definitive than advertised. This was already in my cycle 1 review.
Codex's point that reproducibility is limited (codes available only upon request) is valid, though minor for this type of paper.

main_claims.claim: GreyRing accurately reproduces both amplitude and phase of the frequency-domain post-merger signal, extending prior amplitude-only greybody models.
main_claims.notes: Strong NR validation. The phase model δφ = φ₁/ω is empirically motivated (reducing mismatch by ~3 orders of magnitude over amplitude-only) and interpreted post-hoc as a Laurent expansion. The amplitude has a Green's function grounding in Ref. [111]; the phase does not inherit the same first-principles derivation. Both pieces of the full model are empirically validated.

main_claims.claim: GreyRing typically outperforms state-of-the-art time-domain models (TEOBPM) in mismatch.
main_claims.notes: Comparison uses a noise-unweighted inner product (Eq. 6) internally, while TEOBPM numbers are imported from Ref. [52] which may use a different metric or frequency range. Codex correctly identifies this as an apples-to-oranges comparison. The claimed order-of-magnitude advantage may be inflated by this methodological inconsistency.
main_claims.paper_location: Abstract; main text after Eq. (5); Fig. 2

main_claims.claim: The greybody-factor consistency test infers (M_f, χ_f) from the post-merger signal alone, avoiding overtones and early start-time sensitivity.
main_claims.notes: The start-time problem is genuinely avoided, but the lower frequency cutoff f_low plays an analogous role: phenomenological parameters depend strongly on f_low (Fig. 9), and the authors must identify a 'valid' band (550–650 Hz) empirically. The systematic is shifted from time to frequency domain, not eliminated. Fig. 10 showing stability of M_f, χ_f across f_low is encouraging but does not fully close this concern.
main_claims.paper_location: Tests of gravity with GreyRing; Discussion; Appendix D Figs. 9–10

main_claims.claim: GreyRing applied to GW250114 yields posteriors for (M_f, χ_f) consistent with and slightly narrower than standard LVK ringdown analyses.
main_claims.notes: Fig. 3 clearly shows compatible posteriors. However, most extrinsic parameters (sky position, distance, inclination, polarization) are fixed to LVK values rather than marginalized, artificially tightening the GreyRing posteriors relative to the LVK analysis. The check that inclination/polarization sampling has negligible impact is partial. The 'slightly narrower' claim is therefore not apples-to-apples.
main_claims.paper_location: Application to GW250114; Fig. 3; Appendix D

main_claims.claim: The greybody factor resums all overtones and captures features beyond a QNM expansion, including late-time tails.
main_claims.notes: Formally correct: the Green's function encodes all QNM contributions plus branch-cut tails. However, GreyRing as deployed adds 3 phenomenological parameters per multipole fitted to NR data; the 'resummation' is a structural property of R(ω), not something the specific empirical fit guarantees to capture accurately.

main_claims.claim: GreyRing achieves mismatch accuracy sufficient for ringdown SNR ~ 100–200, relevant for next-generation detectors.
main_claims.notes: Uses standard criterion M < 1/(2 SNR²). With M ~ 10⁻⁴ this gives SNR ~ 70, somewhat below the stated 100–200 range. More importantly, the mismatches are computed with a noise-unweighted inner product while the indistinguishability criterion is derived for the noise-weighted case, creating an inconsistency that Codex correctly flags.

missing_assumptions.assumption: The frequency-band selection criterion (parameters vary by <10% across fitting range; noise plateau <10% of signal amplitude) is heuristic and simulation-dependent. No principled, prior-to-data prescription for selecting the valid band for an arbitrary event is provided.
missing_assumptions.paper_location: Appendix B (Fits to numerical simulations); Appendix D
missing_assumptions.why_it_matters: For a generalizable GR test, the frequency band must be selected without knowledge of the expected answer. The current prescription could be tuned to yield stable results for SXS:BBH:3617/GW250114 but fail for other events or SNR regimes.

missing_assumptions.assumption: Spherical-spheroidal mode mixing is subdominant in the tested frequency range. This is asserted rather than verified quantitatively for GW250114 with χ_f ~ 0.7.
missing_assumptions.paper_location: Appendix B: 'we neglect such mixing, as it is expected to be subdominant in the frequency range of interest'
missing_assumptions.why_it_matters: For rapidly spinning remnants, mode mixing coefficients are non-negligible. Ignoring them when applying the model to GW250114 (χ_f ~ 0.7) could bias the fitted parameters, particularly for subdominant multipoles.

missing_assumptions.assumption: The phenomenological parameters (A_ext, γ, φ₁) cannot absorb genuine beyond-GR signal features while leaving (M_f, χ_f) biased. The test's GR-probing power rests on this assumption.
missing_assumptions.paper_location: Main text: 'A, φ₁, and η are source-dependent fitting parameters'; Appendix D Fig. 11
missing_assumptions.why_it_matters: If a beyond-GR modification alters the overall spectral slope or phase structure in ways degenerate with A_ext or φ₁, the test would fail to detect the deviation. The paper does not assess this degeneracy.

missing_assumptions.assumption: Nonlinear (quadratic QNM) contributions to the ringdown signal are negligible at current SNR levels, so the Kerr linear perturbation theory greybody factor is a sufficient model.
missing_assumptions.why_it_matters: At GW250114 SNR, nonlinear quadratic QNM contributions may be detectable. Mismodeling them as part of the greybody factor signal could bias (M_f, χ_f) in ways that could be falsely interpreted as GR deviations in future high-SNR events.

needs_human_spotcheck.question: Is the mismatch definition used in Ref. [52] (for TEOBPM) identical to the noise-unweighted inner product of Eqs. (5)–(6) in this paper? If they differ in noise weighting or frequency range, the claimed order-of-magnitude improvement over TEOBPM may not be reliable.
needs_human_spotcheck.why_model_cannot_resolve: Ref. [52] (arXiv:2511.02915) is a November 2025 preprint not accessible in my training data at sufficient detail to verify the exact mismatch definition.

needs_human_spotcheck.question: Does Ref. [111] (Rosato, De Amicis, Pani, arXiv:2603.20490) actually prove that the full complex reflection amplitude R(ω) factors out of the Green's function for Kerr (not just Schwarzschild), and does this extend to the comparable-mass merger regime used in GreyRing?
needs_human_spotcheck.why_model_cannot_resolve: Ref. [111] is a March 2026 preprint that may be outside training data. The extension from test-particle perturbation theory to comparable-mass mergers involves empirical assumptions that cannot be verified from the abstract alone.

needs_human_spotcheck.question: Are the SXS simulations used for the GreyRing mismatch validation in Figs. 2 and 5 fully independent of those used to develop or fit the phenomenological parameter prescription in Ref. [113]? Cross-contamination would inflate apparent mismatch performance.
needs_human_spotcheck.why_model_cannot_resolve: The paper specifies simulations above a catalog ID cutoff for Appendix B but does not explicitly state whether the full validation set overlaps with the training set of Ref. [113]'s fitting formulas.

needs_human_spotcheck.question: For GW250114 specifically, what is the impact on the GreyRing (M_f, χ_f) posteriors if all four extrinsic parameters (sky location, distance, inclination, polarization) are jointly marginalized rather than fixed? The paper checks only inclination and polarization separately.
needs_human_spotcheck.why_model_cannot_resolve: This requires running additional Bayesian inference with full extrinsic marginalization, which is a computational task beyond static text analysis.

novelty_assessment: Incremental but meaningful within a well-defined program. The amplitude greybody model was established in Ref. [113] (same group, December 2025); the key new contributions here are: (1) the phase correction δφ = φ₁/ω completing the complex waveform template, (2) the assembled Bayesian inference pipeline, (3) the GR consistency test framing, and (4) the first application to GW250114 real data. The theoretical motivation comes from Ref. [111] (same group, March 2026). Conceptually, this is not a fundamentally new idea—greybody factors in ringdown modeling were established by Refs. [98,99,112]—but the full-phase implementation with its substantial mismatch improvements and the demonstrated Bayesian application are genuine advances. Gemini's novelty score of 9 overstates the advance relative to [113]. I place novelty at 7: solidly useful incremental contribution with practical value for next-generation ringdown analysis.
score_rationale: Technical soundness (7): The amplitude model is well-grounded empirically and via Ref. [111], but the phase model δφ = φ₁/ω is empirical with post-hoc justification. The key theoretical reference [111] is an unreviewed same-group companion preprint. The GW250114 analysis fixes most extrinsic parameters, and the mismatch comparison with TEOBPM uses potentially inconsistent metrics. These are manageable but real soundness caveats. Novelty (7, revised upward from 6): The assembled package—phase completion, Bayesian pipeline, GR consistency test, GW250114 application—is a meaningful contribution beyond the amplitude-only [113] baseline, even if not conceptually revolutionary. Significance (8): Frequency-domain ringdown templates robust to start-time choice address a genuine bottleneck in GR testing, directly applicable to next-generation detectors. Clarity (8): Well-structured letter with appropriate supplemental material. The bibliographic error in Ref. [107] (YASTN vs numpy) is a minor lapse. Reviewer confidence (6): I can audit the logic and structure but cannot independently verify the Green's function calculations in [111] or the NR simulation cross-validation with [113].
summary: GreyRing is a frequency-domain post-merger waveform template built on the Kerr reflection amplitude R(ω), extending predecessor amplitude-only greybody models by adding an empirical phase correction δφ(ω) = φ₁/ω. Validated against aligned-spin SXS simulations with mismatches ~10⁻⁴ for the (2,2) mode, and applied to GW250114 via Bayesian inference. The authors propose a GR consistency test comparing (M_f, χ_f) from GreyRing with standard QNM spectroscopy. After reading the other reviewers, my overall assessment is largely unchanged: this is a well-executed incremental advance with practical value, but several claims need qualification. Gemini's novelty score of 9 is too high given the close lineage to Ref. [113] (same group, Dec 2025). Codex raises legitimate concerns about fixed extrinsic parameters and the frequency-cutoff vs start-time analogy, though elevating both to "major" with verdict-changing weight overstates the severity. I revise novelty slightly upward to 7 to credit the assembled full-pipeline package and GW250114 application.

technical_objections.evidence: Paper states 'The term φ₁/ω is the simplest among a family of parametrizations discussed in the Supplemental Material' and 'can be understood as the leading-order high-frequency contribution in a Laurent expansion.' No derivation from perturbation theory is given.
technical_objections.objection: The phase correction δφ(ω) = φ₁/ω in Eq. (4) is introduced on empirical grounds and justified post-hoc as a Laurent expansion. No derivation from the Green's function framework of Ref. [111] is provided. The model is therefore phenomenological in its phase component, weakening the 'analytical foundation' claim.

technical_objections.evidence: Ref. [111] is cited as 'recently demonstrated' and all phase/amplitude model motivations flow through it. The companion preprint [113] (Dec 2025, same group) also forms much of the amplitude baseline.
technical_objections.objection: The key theoretical foundation—that R(ω) is explicitly encoded in the Green's function for the Kerr ringdown response—rests entirely on Ref. [111] (Rosato, De Amicis, Pani, arXiv:2603.20490, March 2026), a preprint submitted by largely the same author group one month before this paper. This has not been independently verified or peer-reviewed, creating circular dependence in the theoretical justification.

technical_objections.evidence: Fig. 9 shows pronounced variation in A_ext, γ, b₁ with f_low. The paper itself identifies a validity regime empirically rather than providing a principled selection criterion.
technical_objections.objection: The lower frequency cutoff f_low plays an analogous role to the ringdown start time that the method claims to avoid. Appendix D shows strong dependence of phenomenological parameters on f_low, and a 'valid' band (550–650 Hz) must be identified empirically. The claim in the abstract and Discussion that GreyRing avoids start-time ambiguities is overstated.
technical_objections.paper_location: Tests of gravity with GreyRing; Discussion; Appendix D Figs. 9–10

technical_objections.evidence: Appendix D states 'extrinsic parameters (sky location, inclination, polarization, and distance) are fixed to the values reported in Table 2'. Only inclination and polarization were subsequently sampled as a check.
technical_objections.objection: The real-data precision comparison with LVK ringdown analyses is not apples-to-apples: sky location, distance, inclination, and polarization are fixed to LVK values for GreyRing while the LVK analyses marginalize over these. Fixing four extrinsic parameters artificially tightens the GreyRing posteriors. The partial sensitivity check (inclination + polarization only) does not fully validate the claim of 'comparable or slightly better precision'.
technical_objections.paper_location: Application to GW250114; Appendix D; Fig. 3

technical_objections.evidence: Eq. (6) explicitly states 'the inner product is defined without detector-noise weighting.' The Discussion then invokes the Flanagan–Hughes criterion tied to noise-weighted overlaps.
technical_objections.objection: The mismatch is computed with a noise-unweighted inner product (Eq. 6), but the indistinguishability criterion M < 1/(2 SNR²) is derived for noise-weighted overlaps. The inference from unweighted mismatches to parameter-estimation adequacy at specific SNRs is not formally justified and may overstate the model's accuracy at detector-relevant frequency ranges.
technical_objections.paper_location: Validation against numerical simulations, Eqs. (5)–(6); Discussion paragraph on SNR 100–200

technical_objections.evidence: The paper states 'conceptually similar to inspiral–merger–ringdown consistency tests' but does not benchmark the sensitivity of the proposed test to specific GR deviations.
technical_objections.objection: The consistency test as designed compares two estimates of (M_f, χ_f) both derived from the same post-merger frequency-domain data: GreyRing versus QNM spectroscopy. If both models are reasonable fits to Kerr ringdown data, they trivially agree. The test's power to detect GR deviations relative to IMR consistency tests is not quantified.
technical_objections.paper_location: Tests of gravity with GreyRing; Application to GW250114

technical_objections.evidence: The reference list entry [107] cites Rams et al. 2025 (SciPost Phys. Codeb. 52, arXiv:2405.12196) which is a tensor network code, not numpy. Numpy should be Harris et al. 2020.
technical_objections.objection: Reference [107] in the bibliography is listed as 'YASTN: Yet another symmetric tensor networks' (a tensor network library), not numpy, despite the software section listing numpy as a used package. This appears to be a bibliographic error that went unnoticed by all reviewers.
